shortTitle: Pov: You're The Cartels Worst Hitman

content.text: This is you, standing on a stained motel carpet, hands shaking, clutching a prepaid phone you swore you’d smash last time you screwed up. Someone on the second floor is stomping around, probably watching, probably judging—just another day in the world where being invisible is safer than being good. The phone buzzes. Unmistakable. Your boss’s gruff voice—a honeyed accent with the threat of death beneath every syllable. You’re out of chances. Mess up once, they say you’re unlucky. Twice, you’re a clown. Third time? That’s a dead man walking. You get one final job. If you don’t deliver, they won’t have to call again.
content.imageDescription: Interior, dingy motel room. The protagonist stands on a stained carpet, visibly anxious and clutching a cheap prepaid phone. The lighting is harsh from a sickly lamp. Through the thin ceiling, footsteps are heard from the floor above. Emphasize the unease and isolation.

content.text: So why is it always raining when you get these calls, each droplet on the window warped by low, sickly lamp light? The city outside doesn’t care if you choke. Neither does the guy in the leather booth across the room—eyes wiped clean of innocence, probably from crossing someone like you. But you aren’t them. You’re the piece everyone laughs about. The liability with a record for chaos, not carnage. Even the lowlifes have a nickname for you, but nobody says it to your face.
content.imageDescription: Nighttime view out the grimy motel window with rain streaking down the glass, yellowish lamp light distorting everything. Outside, the wet, indifferent city glows dimly. In the background, a hardened man sits alone in a leather booth, expression cold.

content.text: You read the text: rough location, a time, and a face that screams trouble. Only it’s never that easy. The last time you got sent out, you set off an alarm before crossing the street. The time before, you lost your pistol in a fast food bathroom because it slipped under the door. People think hitmen are monsters, but they don’t see the business of swallowing failure by the bucket.
content.imageDescription: Close-up shot of the phone screen displaying terse details of the hit: a street address, a time, and a rough mugshot of the mark. Superimposed faded memories: a flashing alarm, a gun under a bathroom stall door, a frustrated protagonist.

content.text: So you stuff the cheap pistol in your waistband and pull on a windbreaker three sizes too big. Blend in to disappear. That never works; you’ve got the nervous look of someone always one foot off a trapdoor. Heading out, you scan the parking lot—engine ticking, two cars with fogged glass, no faces. Paranoia’s the only thing that’s kept you vertical. That and dumb luck. Mostly dumb luck.
content.imageDescription: Protagonist awkwardly puts a cheap pistol in their waistband, zips up an oversized windbreaker. Their face is anxious, eyes darting. They peer out into an empty, rain-slicked motel parking lot where two nondescript cars sit with fogged windows.

content.text: You start walking, smell of stale fries and exhaust blending with the sharp tang of wet concrete. Focus. Remember the boss’s words: “One last chance, chico.” You want to believe that’s mercy. It’s not. You’re the loose end. If you make this hit, maybe they leave you alone. Or maybe they write your name on their funny little list and call in someone better. Don’t think about it. One job at a time.
content.imageDescription: Wide shot of the protagonist leaving the motel, walking along a rain-drenched city street. Reflected neon lights mix with puddles. A drifting narration visualizes the boss’s stern face and echoed words as memory.

content.text: Your phone buzzes. New message. “Don’t screw this up.” You almost laugh. Too late for pep talks. The building you’re supposed to hit looks like every other box in this city: pale bricks, busted blinds, radio static leaking through cracked windows. You count under your breath. First floor, seventh window—shadow moves. That’s your mark. You check your weapon again. Chambered, safety off. Hands slick. Each finger twitch echoes the bad jobs in your past, but there’s no time for shame.
content.imageDescription: Standing across the street from a dreary apartment building: pale brick, broken blinds, flickering window light. The protagonist stares up, scanning, as a shadow moves inside the seventh window. Their hand nervously checks the pistol again.

content.text: A sudden flash—headlights. Car screeches to a stop nearby. Reflexes take over; you duck behind a dumpster. The air is thick with burnt rubber and your own heartbeat. No one gets out. You realize they’re watching you, mirroring your tension. Do they know? Every bone screams to run, but survival is the slowest game here: do nothing, be nothing. Still, your nerves betray you. You snap your head up. Eye contact. The passenger nods, subtle, scripted. Allies? Or more witnesses? Doesn’t matter. The clock is running.
content.imageDescription: Night street, a car’s intense headlights illuminating the protagonist as they duck behind a graffiti-marked dumpster. Steam rises from the pavement. Inside the car, shadowy occupants watch tensely, one making deliberate eye contact and a subtle nod.

content.text: The plan, if you can call it that, is ugly: wait for the mark to leave, follow, finish quick. No improvisation. You tell yourself, “Don’t improvise,” as if you have a choice. But the guy doesn’t leave. He stands in the window, phone pressed to his ear. Maybe he’s calling in muscle. Maybe he’s calling for pizza. Doesn’t matter. Time slides by in molasses. Every second’s another chance for this to unravel.
content.imageDescription: Protagonist waits in an alley, partially shrouded by shadows, watching the building as the mark stands in the window, talking on the phone. Time feels stretched, the protagonist’s tension increasing.

content.text: You pace in the alley, hands twitching. Try and remember how the old-timers do it. But you’ve seen where bravado gets them: bottom of canals, hands zip-tied. Maybe you’re smarter, or just luckier by a hair. You check your pockets. No gloves. Of course. The one time you could use them. You clench your fists ‘til your nails dig moons in your palm, cold sweat slicking your skin. Your stomach tightens: is this fear, or just the leftover burrito from breakfast?
content.imageDescription: Close-up of anxious hands fidgeting in the dim alley. The protagonist checks empty pockets, sweats, and paces. Implied images of failures: zip-tied hands submerged in water, echoing their intrusive thoughts.

content.text: The mark steps away from the window. Thud of stairwell door. Heart ticking upward, staccato. You step out into damp night, light drizzle turning everything rubbery and uncertain. The streetlamp up ahead flickers, a weak strobe that makes every shadow knife-edged. You see him halfway down the block, hunched, clutching a briefcase too carefully.
content.imageDescription: The mark steps out onto the sidewalk, skittish and mysterious with a briefcase, under the intermittent glow of a flickering streetlamp. The protagonist follows from a cautious distance, rain misting the street.

content.text: You walk faster. Keep your head down. At this speed, you’ll have one shot—literally and metaphorically. You move to cross. Foot catches a crack. You stumble. That’s your luck. Every time. If there’s a banana peel on the road to hell, it’s got your name on it. The mark whips around. Eyes wide. His hand drops to his jacket pocket. Gun? Pepper spray? Lottery ticket? Doesn’t matter. Your hand’s on your waistband.
content.imageDescription: On a slick sidewalk, the protagonist hurries after the mark but stumbles on a crack. The mark stops, alarmed, reaching into his jacket. Both are tense and coiled, action hanging in the balance.

content.text: Time slows. You see everything—steam in the gutter, his shoes scuffed raw, the trembling in his left hand. You’re supposed to act now. But doubt steals your voice. You freeze. Instead of drawing, you cough. Yes, cough. Loud. Wet. Enough to make him pause. He glances over his shoulder. You realize he doesn’t recognize you. This is your advantage—the one that was never there before. You step closer, bottle your nerves, speak: “Hey, wrong block, amigo.” It’s not a threat. It’s a plea for the universe to pause, to give you time.
content.imageDescription: Extreme close-up on the tense face-off: mark's hand trembling, steam curling from the gutter, protagonist hesitating. The awkward, desperate cough breaks the silence; the protagonist steps closer, feigning casualness.

content.text: It listens, for once. The mark sidesteps. For the briefest second, you see his face, not as a target, but as a man. Fear draws you together. The gun’s there—in his hand now. You pull your own, both of you shaking. A beat. No words. Then a cat darts out from under a car, tail up, a comma in your nightmare. The mark flinches, looks away. Reflex, you lunge, both of you go down. Rust, sweat, gun oil. It’s not clean. The shot is wild.
content.imageDescription: Sudden chaos: both men pull guns, hands shaking, faces painted with fear. A stray cat runs between them; instinctively, they dive and struggle on the wet pavement, tangling amidst grime and city debris.

content.text: You blink, disoriented. He’s on top, fist in your teeth. You taste blood—yours, his, doesn’t matter. You bite down, hard. He screams, drops the pistol. You roll, kick. Scramble for your own, fingers slipping. Somehow you win—if you can call it that. You’re pointing a barrel at his chest. He can barely breathe. Neither can you.
content.imageDescription: Close, desperate scuffle. The mark pins the protagonist, smashing a fist into his mouth; the protagonist bites back, blood visible. Guns tumble on the ground. The protagonist barely recovers the weapon, both gasping and battered.

content.text: You ask yourself—Should I even finish him? There’s no honor; you’re not that kind of person. But you’re not here for honor. You’re here because running out of chances means dying like a joke. So you pull the trigger. The gun jams. Typical. Always you. Always a circus.
content.imageDescription: Protagonist, shaking and torn, hesitates with the gun aimed at the mark's chest. He struggles internally, then pulls the trigger—only for the gun to jam. Tension, embarrassment, and frustration simmer in the air.

content.text: He’s got the advantage now, going for your throat. But in the chaos, headlights slice through the alley. The parked car—the one that waited—peels out, tires howling. Two figures leap out, faces covered, guns drawn. They shout in a language of threats you’ve lived with since childhood. Their rounds echo down the brick canyon, forcing you and the mark to dive. For a heartbeat, you’re allies in terror. Then metal flickers and you remember your place: the expendable.
content.imageDescription: All hell breaks loose: masked gunmen rush in, firing as they leap from a car. The protagonist and mark, one-time adversaries, dive for cover together amid muzzle flashes and flying debris.

content.text: The shooters fan out, mechanical. Pros. The target scrambles, you crawl. You know how this ends: loose ends tie themselves up in body bags. You look over—he’s coughing, battered, still alive. You think—in another world maybe you’re not enemies. Too late for second thoughts. You grab the briefcase, hands shaking. Bullets ping off pipes over your head.
content.imageDescription: Shooters move in tactical formation, stalking down the alley. The protagonist, bruised and desperate, grabs the mark’s briefcase and crawls behind a pile of crates as bullets ricochet above them. The mark gasps for breath nearby.

content.text: Somehow, you buck the odds—duck left, out the alley. A fence blocks your way. You scrabble up, knees scraping, briefcase slamming your ribs. Everything burns—lungs, legs, reputation. On the other side, you catch your breath long enough to realize the mark—still alive, limping after you. But now those shooters are behind him, shouting your names.
content.imageDescription: Frantic nighttime escape: protagonist hauls themself over a chain-link fence, briefcase slamming into their side, knees bleeding. On the other side, they gasp in exhaustion as the mark limps after them, shooters in pursuit.

content.text: Should you help him? He’d leave you for dead. But something in your gut won’t let you. Maybe it’s that old urge for redemption—or maybe you’re just tired of losing. You swing the briefcase into the first shooter’s gut as he rounds the fence. He folds. The second fires wild. You’re both on the ground, inching back-to-back like strangers with a secret pact.
content.imageDescription: As the first shooter chases, the protagonist swings the briefcase into his stomach, dropping him. Wild shots from a second gunman as the protagonist and mark crouch back-to-back on wet concrete, improvised allies.

content.text: He’s got family, he gasps. What do you have? A phone, two bullets, and the weariness of failure. There’s a moment—just a moment—where the desperation binds you. You jam the fresh magazine home, offer him the pistol. He shakes his head. “You finish it.”
content.imageDescription: Close shot: both men grimy and winded, talking breathlessly. The mark confesses he has family; protagonist reloads the pistol and offers it to him. The mark, shaken, refuses and pushes it back.

content.text: You do, but not the way bosses wanted. Two shots send the hunters crawling. You high-tail it out, gasping, dragging the mark with you. Not heroic. Just suicidal luck. Your only reward? A call from the boss an hour later: “You missed the point.”
content.imageDescription: Two loud gunshots force the wounded shooters to withdraw. The protagonist half-drags, half-supports the limping mark down a darkened alley. Later, the protagonist, alone and exhausted, answers their buzzing phone to a terse rebuke.

content.text: That’s your life on repeat. Never clean, always sideways. Every narrow escape makes you more of a legend to the desperate, more of a liability to the organization. You get sent on cleanup jobs, supposed to finish the sick, the weak, the ones who already know they’ve lost. Each gig is a new humiliation—dropping a weapon down a sewer, having to threaten a cabbie for a ride when your getaway car gets towed, showing up to an address where the victim’s already gone.
content.imageDescription: A rough montage: protagonist's screw-ups on repeat—dropping a weapon into a sewer grate, angrily bargaining with a cab driver, arriving late to an empty safehouse. Sense of frustration and mockery from others.

content.text: Every failed contract is another coil in the vice. You start seeing knives in peoples’ smiles, hear threats woven into radio static. You eat ramen, sleep with your shoes on, trust no one—not even the mirror. You develop tricks: take the stairs, never the elevator. Sit near exits. Talk only in half-truths. Always know where to run, because soon you will have to.
content.imageDescription: Nightly scenes of solitary living: protagonist eats instant noodles, sits on a creaky bed fully clothed, eyes warily tracking shadowy reflections. Quick flashes: using stairs, sitting near doors, glancing suspiciously at those nearby.

content.text: But the jobs keep coming, and the screw-ups pile up: the time you try to plant a tracker but tag the wrong car, sparking a turf war—a pizza delivery boy ends up shot, and somehow that’s on you. Or the night you’re told to tail a rival, only to find you’ve been set up; an empty apartment, and two shooters waiting in the shadows, their laughter cold and practiced as yours is desperate. You pay them off with your last stash of cash. They take it, spit at your feet, and let you crawl away.
content.imageDescription: A montage: the protagonist plants a tracker on a car (revealed to be a pizza delivery vehicle), chaos erupts; then, protagonist walks into a trap—an empty warehouse, two armed assassins lurking. Desperate, they hand over a bulging envelope; one spits in contempt.

content.text: You wonder—why do you keep getting sent out? Is it because you’re easy to blame? Or do you offer some entertainment in a world so dead-eyed and bleak that even failure becomes a kind of sport? The whispers grow. You cross into different clubs and men nudge each other, glance away when you look too long. No respect, just pity, or worse, fear of catching the disease of your bad luck.
content.imageDescription: Protagonist walking into shadowy clubs; patrons nudge one another and look away, nobody meeting their gaze. The tension and reputation are palpable, whispers and sidelong glances following them everywhere.

content.text: So why do you keep answering the phone? Because you know what happens when you don’t. Someone finds you, in a bathroom stall or a bus stop, their grin sharp with purpose. You’ve seen what happens to people who refuse assignments. Not pretty. So you keep moving, keep trying, every job becoming a new kind of purgatory.
content.imageDescription: A sequence: protagonist sits on a city bus and in a grimy public restroom, always glancing over their shoulder. Someone approaches—sharp smile, subtle threat. Briefly cut to the aftermath for those who refused: bloodstain or empty chair.

content.text: One day, a kid follows you home. You spot the glint of a blade in his pocket. He’s shaking, nervous—first-timer. He tries to rob you; you laugh, but it comes out a strangled bark. He wants your wallet. You hand it over. Why fight? Nothing left to take. But then, he whispers, "It’s not the money. It’s the message." He’s been sent. It was bound to happen. Every shadow is loaded with meaning, every silence a warning. You thank him—sincerely. His confusion is brief. He runs.
content.imageDescription: Protagonist caught in an alley near home; tense, a nervous kid with a hidden knife tries to mug them. Protagonist gives up their wallet, then the kid delivers a hushed message. An oddly sincere thank you leaves the kid baffled, fleeing into shadow.

content.text: The next job isn’t a hit. It’s a test. You’re ordered to collect money, a simple pickup they say. But the shop is empty, dust floating where sunlight should be, cash upstairs on a desk. You spot the trap too late: two thugs, bored and slow, lounging by the rear exit. You try to talk your way out and almost succeed. Then your phone rings, loud, your ringtone a bad joke. They tense. Weapons flash. You realize you can’t win. You smile anyway—the nervous reflex of someone with nothing left. The oldest trick: surprise yourself. You throw the briefcase, sprint, weave between fire escapes. Bullets skim your jacket. Still standing. Still the worst. Still alive.
content.imageDescription: Protagonist in a dim, dusty abandoned shop; light filters down, empty but for an envelope of cash. By the back exit, two goons block the way. As the tense standoff peaks, a loud ringtone interrupts; guns are drawn. Protagonist grins, then flings the briefcase and bolts through a warren of fire escapes as bullets fly, narrowly escaping.

content.text: But something’s shifted. The jobs dry up. Calls become shorter, fewer words. Messenger boys show up at cafes where you eat, quietly staring, always two at a time. Your reputation outlives your usefulness. You know you have a week, maybe less. Each night, sleep comes hard. Memories crowd in—faces, threats, muffled gunshots, the taste of battery acid fear. You question—how far are you willing to sink to crawl out alive?
content.imageDescription: A somber montage: protagonist sitting alone in a shabby cafe, two silent men at a nearby table, watching. At night, the protagonist lies in a cramped bed, haunted by flashbacks—gunfire, threats, faces spinning past in the dim.

content.text: On the sixth day, an envelope slides under your door. No return address. Just an address and a warning: “End it, or it ends you.”
content.imageDescription: Low-angle shot inside a seedy apartment: a white envelope slides under the chipped door. On the envelope, an address and a single line: a cold ultimatum.

content.text: So you go, because you have nothing else left. You dress in black, shoes tied tight, knife tucked along your spine. The address leads you to an old warehouse, its walls bleeding rust. Inside, the sharp tang of gasoline, sweat, and despair. Figures wait, angry silhouettes against the floodlight. It’s the boss’s lieutenants. Guns unslung, fingers resting heavy.
content.imageDescription: Night. Protagonist, wearied and resigned, pulls on black clothes and laces up tightly. The destination: a vast, rotting warehouse fringed with rust. Inside, under harsh floodlights, armed henchmen wait in tense formation.

content.text: You realize this is it. No marks. No pats on the back. Just the end waiting to be tied up, easy and quiet. You laugh—a choke, bitter. “Took you long enough.”
content.imageDescription: Abandoned warehouse interior. Protagonist stands alone across from a semicircle of boss’s gunmen. They deliver their “final” verdict. The protagonist, defiant to the last, lets out a bitter, broken laugh.

content.text: But the world loves chaos. Sirens wail outside, blue lights cutting through the gloom. Someone tipped the cops. The lieutenants curse, scatter, vaulting over crates and ducking under steel doors. You slither out a back window, legs pumping, every muscle blazing. Freedom is inertia—the longer you move, the longer you live.
content.imageDescription: Chaos erupts: flashing blue police lights strobe through the warehouse windows. Gangsters scramble for escape, vaulting crates and fleeing as sirens blare. The protagonist slips through a cracked window, escaping into the night.

content.text: You tumble into the street, cut across alleys, shoes pounding the heartbeat of a dying city. You keep going until dawn, collapsing in an abandoned playground. You laugh until you can’t. Is this victory? Survival by mistake?
content.imageDescription: Early dawn. Protagonist, disheveled and exhausted, runs breathlessly through deserted alleyways and city streets until he finally drops, laughing bitterly, alone on the swings of a desolate playground.

content.text: The phone rings again, but you let it buzz, let it fall silent on the cold ground. You know you should run—change your face, disappear to the border, melt into the endless crowds of failures. Maybe that’s your edge, the one nobody saw coming. You didn’t break. You bent, and bent again, until the old you snapped off, and only stubborn, ugly survival remained.
content.imageDescription: Lying in the empty playground, the protagonist’s phone buzzes with an incoming call on the cold ground; this time he doesn’t answer. He stares at the sky in weary contemplation, finally at peace with his battered resilience.

content.text: The joke’s on them. The worst hitman? Maybe. Or maybe just the last one left standing, too broken to fear the end, too stubborn to die on cue. Every shadow holds a threat, every heartbeat a question: Do you vanish, or do you claw your way into legend, not with precision, not with terror, but with the dumb luck of the forever underestimated?
content.imageDescription: Montage of ghostly shadows cast around the playground as night turns to dawn. The protagonist, battered but upright on the swing, resolved and alone, becomes almost mythic—an unlikely survivor in a world built to outlast him.

content.text: You exhale, lungs raw, hands trembling but empty. The city sprawls out, silent, and for a moment, you feel invisible in a world too loud to care. You survived. Nobody’s applauding. Nobody’s hunting. Just the cold dawn, a playground swing, and the tiniest sliver of peace—until the next call.
content.imageDescription: Wide shot: protagonist alone on a swing as dawn breaks, city skyline in the distance, breathing in the sharp morning air. He looks small and invisible in the sprawling urban silence, savoring a fleeting, hard-won peace.

protagonist.description: The protagonist stands on a stained carpet, visibly anxious and clutching a cheap prepaid phone.
